title: "How to read a screening statistic" explainer (education only)
context: M3 is hard-gated on securing both a credentialed oncology clinician and a patient advocate as blocking reviewers. This explainer is the foundational plain-language piece that must ship before indicator-specific trend explainers (task 015). It teaches population-level data literacy for screening statistics: what a coverage rate is, what basis (self-report/measured/model-based) means, what a confidence interval conveys, how to read a trend, and what these statistics do not say. It is general information and education only — never medical advice, never a personalized recommendation. The persistent 'not medical advice — consult your care team' notice is mandatory and non-negotiable. Cannot start until oncology clinician AND patient-advocate reviewers are secured.
objective: Write the 'how to read a screening statistic' plain-language educational explainer: covering what a screening coverage rate is, the meaning of basis (self-report/measured/model-based), confidence intervals, trends, and what population-level statistics do not say about individuals. Must pass the full misread checklist and carry the persistent 'not medical advice' notice. Requires oncologist + advocate sign-off (both, blocking) before publication.
acceptanceCriteria: ENTRY GATE: oncology clinician AND patient-advocate reviewers both secured (named, confirmed) before this task starts; task is blocked until both are in place

Explainer is plain-language education only; explains population-level screening coverage statistics (what a rate is, self-report vs. measured vs. model-based, confidence intervals, trend interpretation) without giving any personalized recommendation, eligibility ruling, or diagnosis guidance
Carries a persistent, prominent 'This is general information, not medical advice — consult your care team' notice on every page/section; notice is not buried in a footnote

Passes the full misread checklist: denominator stated and explained; basis (self-report/measured/model-based) labeled and explained; model-based small-area estimates flagged as estimates not counts; no causal verbs used on associational trend data; no advice verbs; guideline bodies cited with name, version, and date where referenced
No-causal-verb lint passes: no claim that a trend 'caused' or 'led to' any outcome; language is explicitly correlational and descriptive
Not-medical-advice lint passes: no language telling readers what screening to seek, when to get screened, or whether they should be screened
WCAG 2.2 AA accessibility requirements met; plain-language readability target met (target reading level appropriate for the intended public audience)
Comprehension check conducted: >= 80% correct on pre-registered 'what this rate does/doesn't say' check items (advocate panel or small reader test)
Oncologist sign-off recorded (clinical accuracy, no advice-creep) — BLOCKING
Patient-advocate sign-off recorded (comprehension, tone, patient-serving framing) — BLOCKING
output: A published 'how to read a screening statistic' plain-language educational explainer: misread checklist passed, persistent 'not medical advice' notice present, WCAG 2.2 AA met, comprehension check >= 80%; both oncologist and patient-advocate sign-offs recorded (blocking); no personalized recommendation, eligibility ruling, or diagnosis guidance.
